EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary cold front and upper level trough brought scattered severe thunderstorms to southeast Virginia in the late afternoon and evening. These storms mainly produced large hail, with a few reports of golf ball size hail and larger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 1 inch diameter hail and crop damage just north of Cape Charles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid to upper level disturbance tracked east across the central and southern Appalachians, coinciding with the peak heating and instability during the afternoon hours. Some storms first developed along the Blue Ridge prior to the convection associated with the upper trough, while storms associated with the trough tracked into the area from the west during the mid to late afternoon. Moderate instability with MUCAPE in the 1000-1500 J/kg, and 0-6km shear was between 35-40 knots, with lapse rate increasing to 7 C/km by the afternoon, so all parameters for severe weather were favorable, and both wind damage and hail were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large surface low pressure system slowly moved across the northern Plains, with the associated cold front stalling across the Ohio Valley. A mesoscale convective system developed over Kentucky and tracked east towards the central Appalachians, bringing storms the area by the afternoon of May 16. The cold pool from the advancing MCS provided enough lift for convection to break through the morning inversion, and plenty of daytime heating increased SBCAPE to 4000 J/kg. The mesoscale environment was conducive for large hail and damaging winds, which led to numerous reports of downed trees and hail an inch or larger in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were downed by thunderstorm winds on Mill Creek Road near Boxwood Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving closed upper level low originating from the Gulf of America brought anomalous moisture into the area. A long duration period of rain ensued, with rain rates exceeding two inches an hour during the afternoon and evening of May 13th. The slow moving storms coupled with training led to localized rainfall totals of four to eight inches across the Blue Ridge, with lesser amounts of two to four inches along and west of I-95. Flooding lingered into the morning hours of May 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure (992 mb) moved across the Great Lakes on April 14. Isolated storms tracked across West Virginia and into Virginia during the evening. One of these storms produced large hail and damaging winds as it moved across portions of central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported a tree knocked down due to the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the midst of a passing low pressure system, strong thunderstorms crossed into southwest Virginia on the evening of April 14th. These storms were notorious for producing very large hail as they traveled through the Ohio Valley and into West Virginia throughout the afternoon, and this bled into Buchanan County with a single observance of ping pong sized hail occurring in Hurley. Activity then quickly pressed eastward into central Virginia for the remainder of the evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees blew down around New Hope.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough over the Midwest and surface low near the Great Lakes brought a robust cold front into the Mid Atlantic region. A strongly-forced area of linear convection moved from west to east across portions of central into eastern Virginia. These storms mostly produced strong to severe straight line wind gusts but one waterspout moved ashore from the York River into Gloucester county, causing EF0 damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down in Middlesex county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed over the City of Roanoke just before 6:45 pm EDT on August 21st, initially producing rainfall rates to nearly 5 per hour. Though the storm gradually weakened, it remained anchored over primarily the western half of the city through around 8:15 pm EDT before finally pushing away to the south.  While nearly all of the city received at least 2 inches of rain, the highest totals were over the Peters Creek basin, where a 4 to 5 bullseye stretched from the Roanoke-Blacksburg Regional Airport (KROA) westward to S. Electric Road.  Both the airport and a personal weather station observed rainfall totals at the 1-hour and 2-hour durations that were 500-year rainfall events per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates. By 8:00 pm EDT, CREST Unit Streamflow near the airport had peaked at just over 1,600 cfs/mi^2, with much of the western half of the city observing values ranging from 550 to 950 cfs/mi^2. FLASH 1-hour Average Recurrence Intervals peaked near Electric Drive at just under a 200-year event at 7:46 pm EDT.  Due to the rapid flooding of Peters Creek Road that runs alongside Peters Creek, the occupants of numerous vehicles required swift water rescues from emergency services personnel.  A Flash Flood Warning with a Base tag was issued at 7:29 pm EDT for the City of Roanoke, which was upgraded to a warning with a Considerable tag at 7:52 pm EDT when reports of swift water rescues began to come in.  After coordinating with the Emergency Manager of the City of Roanoke, the warning was upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency with a Catastrophic tag at 9:02 pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from 4 to 5 of rainfall was observed flowing several inches deep across Ring Road near the Valley View Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed over the City of Roanoke just before 6:45 pm EDT on August 21st, initially producing rainfall rates to nearly 5 per hour. Though the storm gradually weakened, it remained anchored over primarily the western half of the city through around 8:15 pm EDT before finally pushing away to the south.  While nearly all of the city received at least 2 inches of rain, the highest totals were over the Peters Creek basin, where a 4 to 5 bullseye stretched from the Roanoke-Blacksburg Regional Airport (KROA) westward to S. Electric Road.  Both the airport and a personal weather station observed rainfall totals at the 1-hour and 2-hour durations that were 500-year rainfall events per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates. By 8:00 pm EDT, CREST Unit Streamflow near the airport had peaked at just over 1,600 cfs/mi^2, with much of the western half of the city observing values ranging from 550 to 950 cfs/mi^2. FLASH 1-hour Average Recurrence Intervals peaked near Electric Drive at just under a 200-year event at 7:46 pm EDT.  Due to the rapid flooding of Peters Creek Road that runs alongside Peters Creek, the occupants of numerous vehicles required swift water rescues from emergency services personnel.  A Flash Flood Warning with a Base tag was issued at 7:29 pm EDT for the City of Roanoke, which was upgraded to a warning with a Considerable tag at 7:52 pm EDT when reports of swift water rescues began to come in.  After coordinating with the Emergency Manager of the City of Roanoke, the warning was upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency with a Catastrophic tag at 9:02 pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peters Creek was observed flowing several feet out of its banks due to flooding caused by 4 to 5 of rain within a 2-hour period.  The creek was observed flowing across Northwood Drive NW up to 2 feet deep, with water flooding across the Audi Auto Dealership lot.  Minor water intrusion occurred within the dealership showroom.  Further north, Peters Creek Road was flooded more than 3 feet deep in spots due to flooding of Peters Creek, with social media video showing multiple vehicles stranded in the water. At least 12 swift water rescues were reported for the occupants stranded in their vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An afternoon of isolated to scattered thunderstorms took place across southwest Virginia on June 28th as a slow moving frontal boundary stumbled around the area. The location of the boundary and strong daytime heating resulted in storms to first develop into West Virginia before sinking down into Dickenson and Buchanan Counties. The storms that ventured into Virginia remained sub-severe and imposed singular tree damage in Dickenson County before activity weakened more and moved out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One downed tree was observed on Dickenson Highway in Clinchco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Hurricane Erin stayed several hundred miles east of the Middle Atlantic coast on August 21, 2025. However, due the extremely large size of the cyclone with an expansive wind field, tropical storm force gusts occurred over the Atlantic, Chesapeake Bay, and far eastern and southeastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although Hurricane Erin passed well offshore on August 21, the wind field was so large that northerly winds gusted to 35 to 45 miles per hour from 2 AM EST through 3 PM EST. The peak gust was 44 mph at 4:56 AM EST at the Virginia Beach ASOS and 43 mph at the Cape Henry NOS site around 5 AM EST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although Hurricane Erin passed well offshore on August 21, the wind field was so large that northerly winds gusted to 30 to 40 miles per hour from 6 AM EST until 3 PM EST. The peak gust was 40 mph around 9 AM EST at the Norfolk International Airport ASOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive, yet localized, damage was reported in Rice with multiple trees snapped and blown over. At least ten large trees were reported to have been snapped or blown over including a 30 foot tall red oak which was snapped above the trunk. Additionally, the fallen trees crushed three vehicles and a tractor as well as caused damage to a roof. The damaging winds were reported to have been very brief, lasting less than a minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across the Ohio Valley on June 8. A stationary front attached to the low was located across central to southeast Virginia. Meanwhile, a secondary surface low formed along the stationary front during the evening of June 8. Scattered storms developed during the afternoon and evening of June 8 and produced locally heavy rain and flooding across portions of central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported by the public in Kingsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm formed along a stationary front across southeast Virginia during the afternoon of June 9. This storm was rotating and produced a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter and broadcast media each reported and provided pictures of a funnel  cloud in the vicinity of US-17 southwest of the Chesapeake airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough approached the Mid-Atlantic region from the west on June 19th. Strong solar heating and rich low level moisture combined for robust instability across the area. Moderate wind shear ahead of the upper trough resulted in sufficient shear for thunderstorm organization and resulted in widespread damaging winds across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on Stony Point Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure wedge was situated over the region as a surface low pressure system developed over the southern Plains, with an expansive area of precipitation that shifted eastward into the Mid Atlantic during the day of February 11. Precipitation began as snow, but with the high pressure wedge in place and warm air increasing aloft, a transition from snow to sleet and freezing rain occurred along and south of the Virginia and North Carolina border, so parts of southern Virginia received significant ice accumulations. Ice accumulations on trees and power lines resulted in thousands of power outages and road closures. Western Virginia, especially the Alleghany Highlands area, received heavy snow, accumulations of a foot or more were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 inches of snow fell in Floyd County. In addition, ice accumulations of up to two thirds of an inch were reported, with the heaviest amounts near Pilot. Around 9000 people in the county lost power. Key Impacts: power outages, downed trees, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough was observed exiting the southern Rockies during the morning of February 15th, with southwesterly windflow ahead of the trough allowing for a fetch of deep moisture from the western Gulf of America. This moisture pooled along a warm front situated across from Kentucky into southwest Virginia, where precipitable water values increased from 0.6 to 0.7 inches at 7 am that morning, to around 1.1 to 1.2 inches by early afternoon, which would be around 2 standard deviations above normal for mid-February. While the warm front shifted northward to the west of the Appalachians, high pressure off the Atlantic coast that was wedged against the eastern face of the Appalachians delayed the northward progress of the warm front. As such, the front remained stationary, and provided the focus for training of bands of heavy rain that fell across southwest Virginia through much of the day on February 15th. The rain fell at rates of 0.3 to 0.5 per hour beginning at around 9 am EST in the morning, which persisted for a 9-10 hour period. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 3 inches across southwest Virginia during this period. Soils across the area were nearly saturated from 2 rounds of widespread precipitation that fell earlier in the month. Because MRMS was underestimating rainfall amounts, both the FLASH ARI and QPE/FFG values were also significantly underestimated. In these situation, monitoring ground-truth precipitation totals from a network of private weather stations might have prompted Flash Flood Warnings to be issued sooner.

This event is a 10-year and a 25-year event (10%-4% Annual Exceedance Probability) per USGS StreamStats.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clinch River at Richlands, VA (RLRV2), crested at the Major Flood Stage (16.0 feet) of 16.09 feet at 11:30 pm EST on the 15th above the Minor Flood Stage of 10.0 feet. This is tied for the 4th-highest crest of all-time (Records dating back at least to 1901), and about the highest crest at this gage since 1978. The flooding was the result of between 2.75 to 3.25 inches of rain falling across the entire headwaters during a 9 hour period due to training heavy showers/storms. This event is about a 25-year event (4% Annual Exceedance Probability) per USGS StreamStats.  Significant flooding was observed across town, with water entering many businesses and residences.  As the river rose, multiple properties became inundated along Edgewater Drive near the gage.  Water entered homes along E 1st and 2nd Streets on the east side of town.  Water also surrounded homes on Orange and Jewell Streets, though it is unclear if water did enter any of the homes.  Significant inundation occurred along Alleghany and Road and 4th Street, with multiple businesses along Front Street taking in at least 1 foot of water. Flooding of the river was also observed at the Richlands Tabernacle Church near the intersection of 4th Street and Rockbridge Street. Multiple motor homes around Page and Allen Streets were surrounded by at least 1.5 feet of water, though it did not appear that water entered any of the residences.  In the Raven area, water surrounded several homes on Kirby Road, Atmore Street and Bottom Road, though there were no reports of the water actually entering the homes.  If true, this would be about as high as water could rise without doing so.  Water crested on Kirby Road next to the Doran Grocery Store before receding again.  The elementary school at the end of Bottom Road was surrounded by water.  Water advanced across York and Daw Roads off of Bottom Road as well.  The National Guard was brought in during the flooding to help people evacuate, with at least 51 people assisted from their homes by swift water rescue teams.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wolf Creek near Narrow, VA (WOLV2) crested at 12.85 feet (12,547 cfs) at 1:15 am EST on the 16th above the Moderate Flood Stage of 12 feet (Minor Flood Stage is 10 feet). This was caused by between 2.5 and 2.75 inches of rain falling over a 9 hour period ending at around 7:00 pm on the 15th. Modest snowmelt was a factor given the 2 to locally 4 inches of snow and ice that fell across the basin 2-3 days prior. Soils were partially frozen and saturated from recent heavy precipitation, and stream levels were already running high given rounds of heavy precipitation earlier in the month. This is the highest crest of this gage since March 2010, and the 5th highest documented crest since 1909. This event is about a 25-year flood (4% Annual Exceedance Probability) per USGS StreamStats.  One of the biggest noted impacts was the significant number of basements of homes that were flooded and needed to be pumped dry in the Narrows area. One water rescue was reported along the 4300 block of Wolf Creek Road as water from Wolf Creek surrounded the home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tom's creek was observed rising rapidly out of its banks at multiple locations.  Water was observed flowing across Coal Bank Hollow Road near Highway 460, and flooded the nearby Montgomery County Waste Site, causing damage to one of the service buildings located there.  Further downstream, water was also observed flowing across Tom's Creek Road several inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure moved along a stalled frontal boundary in northern Virginia. This spawned showers and thunderstorms that resulted in localized amounts of two to four inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gauge on Accotink Creek at Strathmeade Springs reached minor flood stage. Woodburn Road was covered by approximately six inches of water at its lowest point. Portions of the Cross County Trail were also flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closed due to high water at least twelve inches deep flowing across the road near intersection of Tatum Road and Shirley Road. Other roads closed nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across the Ohio Valley on June 8. A stationary front attached to the low was located across central to southeast Virginia. Meanwhile, a secondary surface low formed along the stationary front during the evening of June 8. Scattered storms developed during the morning along a remnant MCV and during the afternoon and evening of June 8. These storms produced damaging winds and hail as they moved across portions of central and eastern Virginia. Occasional rotating was also noted as storms interacted with the stationary front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter sized hail (one inch in diameter) 1 mile north of Cowie Corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed along a surface trough and in a hot and humid airmass on June 26. An isolated storm produced minor flash flooding in the City of Richmond in the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported water over the roadway at Chamberlayne Avenue and Brookland Parkway via Waze. This report was corroborated by a VDOT camera on I-95 at mile marker 78.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed along a surface trough and in a hot and humid airmass on June 26. An isolated storm produced minor flash flooding in the City of Richmond in the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported flooding along Atlee Road at I-295.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed along a surface trough and in a hot and humid airmass on June 26. These storms produced damaging winds in the afternoon and evening hours across southern and central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported multiple trees and power lines down in the vicinity of Dill Road and US Route 360.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed along a surface trough and in a hot and humid airmass on June 26. These storms produced damaging winds in the afternoon and evening hours across southern and central Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility company reported a tree downed onto a line at Cold Harbor Road at I-295.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough approached the Mid-Atlantic region from the west on June 19th. Strong solar heating and rich low level moisture combined for robust instability across the area. Moderate wind shear ahead of the upper trough resulted in sufficient shear for thunderstorm organization and resulted in widespread damaging winds across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS station KBKT at Blackstone Fort reported a wind gust to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted portions of eastern and central Virginia over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105-115 degrees with high temperatures in the upper 90s to around 100 degrees and dew points in the upper 70s. Dew points increased to around 80 degrees locally in the evenings. Given the duration and magnitude of the heatwave, little relief occurred overnight between each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted the city of Newport News over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105 to 115 degrees with most areas peaking around 110 degrees. Daily high temperatures in the upper 90s and dew points in the mid to upper 70s were also common. Dew points peaked around 80 degrees in some locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle with a single occupant was pushed off of Kimberling Road into deeper water as Kimberling Creek flooded at least 2 feet over the roadway. The occupant attempted to abandon the vehicle, but was swept away by the flooding and passed away. Nearby, Wesendonck Road was observed flooded and impassible at several locations due to multiple creeks overflowing their banks. This includes Nobusiness Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southwestern Virginia during the afternoon and evening of June 19. This front interacted with a very unstable environment during the peak heating of the day. The presence of moderate shear allowed an organized line of strong to severe thunderstorms to form and cause damaging thunderstorm winds around much of southwestern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds across Bethlehem Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southwestern Virginia during the afternoon and evening of June 19. This front interacted with a very unstable environment during the peak heating of the day. The presence of moderate shear allowed an organized line of strong to severe thunderstorms to form and cause damaging thunderstorm winds around much of southwestern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds across Floyd Highway North.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm stalled over portions of Augusta County resulting in a localized one to three inches of rain. This produced some minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closed due to high water around the 200 block of Wayne Ave in Stuarts Draft. Other reports of minor flooding reported in Stuarts Draft as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across the Ohio Valley on June 8. A stationary front attached to the low was located across central to southeast Virginia. Meanwhile, a secondary surface low formed along the stationary front during the evening of June 8. Scattered storms developed during the morning along a remnant MCV and during the afternoon and evening of June 8. These storms produced damaging winds and hail as they moved across portions of central and eastern Virginia. Occasional rotating was also noted as storms interacted with the stationary front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated to be around the size of quarters (one inch in diameter) was observed from a VDOT camera at mile marker 17 on I-95 North.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough approached the Mid-Atlantic region from the west on June 19th. Strong solar heating and rich low level moisture combined for robust instability across the area. Moderate wind shear ahead of the upper trough resulted in sufficient shear for thunderstorm organization and resulted in widespread damaging winds across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell in the roadway on Rt. 628 in the vicinity of Richmond Rd and US 360.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted portions of eastern and central Virginia over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105-115 degrees with high temperatures in the upper 90s to around 100 degrees and dew points in the upper 70s. Dew points increased to around 80 degrees locally in the evenings. Given the duration and magnitude of the heatwave, little relief occurred overnight between each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted Nottoway County over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105 to 115 degrees with most areas peaking around 110 degrees. Daily high temperatures in the upper 90s and dew points in the mid to upper 70s were also common. Dew points peaked around 80 degrees in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough was observed exiting the southern Rockies during the morning of February 15th, with southwesterly windflow ahead of the trough allowing for a fetch of deep moisture from the western Gulf of America. This moisture pooled along a warm front situated across from Kentucky into southwest Virginia, where precipitable water values increased from 0.6 to 0.7 inches at 7 am that morning, to around 1.1 to 1.2 inches by early afternoon, which would be around 2 standard deviations above normal for mid-February. While the warm front shifted northward to the west of the Appalachians, high pressure off the Atlantic coast that was wedged against the eastern face of the Appalachians delayed the northward progress of the warm front. As such, the front remained stationary, and provided the focus for training of bands of heavy rain that fell across southwest Virginia through much of the day on February 15th. The rain fell at rates of 0.3 to 0.5 per hour beginning at around 9 am EST in the morning, which persisted for a 9-10 hour period. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 3 inches across southwest Virginia during this period. Soils across the area were nearly saturated from 2 rounds of widespread precipitation that fell earlier in the month. Additionally, the rain was falling over ground that was partially frozen, and covered in snow and ice from a winter storm that impacted southwest and central Virginia a few days earlier, increasing the rapid runoff potential into the creeks and rivers. In this event, CREST Unit Streamflow did not show impressive returns, while MRMS underestimated some of the precipitation totals. It was those factors combined with the high runoff potential of the rainfall that resulted in a lack of realization how quickly river levels were rising, and just how intense the flooding was becoming, even as far east as Appomattox County. CREST Hydrophobic Unit Flow proved to be a more accurate tool in this situation given the frozen ground, with returns as high as 480 cfs/m^2 across Giles County, which is where some of the most intense flooding was observed. Because MRMS was underestimating rainfall amounts, both the FLASH ARI and QPE/FFG values were also significantly underestimated. In these situation, monitoring ground-truth precipitation totals from a network of private weather stations might have prompted Flash Flood Warnings to be issued sooner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dunlap Creek at near Covington, VA (DLPV2), crested at 11.10 feet (8,209 cfs) at 2:15 am EST on the 16th above the Moderate Flood stage of 11.0 feet. The flooding was the result of between 1.5 and 2.25 inches of rain falling across the basin in about 10 hours ending at 7:30 pm on the 15th. This is the 10th highest documented crest for this gage with records dating back at least to 1929, and is the highest crest of this gage since June 2016.  This event is nearly a 5-year flood (20% Annual Exceedance Probability) per USGS StreamStats.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roanoke River at Lafayette, VA (LAAV2) crested at 11.36 feet (11,800 cfs) at 9:40 am EST on the 15th above the Moderate Flood Stage of 11 feet (Minor Flood Stage is 8 feet). This was caused by between 2.5 and 2.75 inches of rain falling over a 9 hour period ending at around 7:00 pm on the 15th. Modest snowmelt was a factor given the 2 to locally 4 inches of snow and ice that fell across the basin 2-3 days prior. Soils were partially frozen and saturated from recent heavy precipitation, and stream levels were already running high given rounds of heavy precipitation earlier in the month. This is the highest crest of this gage since the flooding associated with remnants of Hurricane Michael in October 2018, and the 12th highest documented crest since 1944. This event is between a 5-year and a 10-year flood (20%-10% Annual Exceedance Probability) per USGS StreamStats.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure spawned showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of June 26th. These thunderstorms were slow moving and produced localized rainfall amounts of two to four inches in an hour or two, which resulted in flash flooding. Another round of showers and thunderstorms developed late in the overnight and into the morning of June 27th bringing additional heavy rain and flooding to Prince William County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swiftwater rescue in Dumfries near Possum Point Road and US-1.

As such, the front remained stationary, and provided the focus for training of bands of heavy rain that fell across southwest Virginia through much of the day on February 15th. The rain fell at rates of 0.3 to 0.5 per hour beginning at around 9 am EST in the morning, which persisted for a 9-10 hour period. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 3 inches across southwest Virginia during this period. Soils across the area were nearly saturated from 2 rounds of widespread precipitation that fell earlier in the month. Additionally, the rain was falling over ground that was partially frozen, and covered in snow and ice from a winter storm that impacted southwest and central Virginia a few days earlier, increasing the rapid runoff potential into the creeks and rivers. In this event, CREST Unit Streamflow did not show impressive returns, while MRMS underestimated some of the precipitation totals. It was those factors combined with the high runoff potential of the rainfall that resulted in a lack of realization how quickly river levels were rising, and just how intense the flooding was becoming, even as far east as Appomattox County. CREST Hydrophobic Unit Flow proved to be a more accurate tool in this situation given the frozen ground, with returns as high as 480 cfs/m^2 across Giles County, which is where some of the most intense flooding was observed. Because MRMS was underestimating rainfall amounts, both the FLASH ARI and QPE/FFG values were also significantly underestimated. In these situation, monitoring ground-truth precipitation totals from a network of private weather stations might have prompted Flash Flood Warnings to be issued sooner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Potts Creek near Covington, VA (PTCV2), crested at 9.67 feet (6,971 cfs) at 7:15 am EST on the 16th above the Minor Flood Stage of 9.0 feet. The flooding was the result of between 1.50 and 2.75 inches of rain falling in a roughly 10-hour period from around 9 am to 7 pm on the 15th. This is the highest crest of this gage since the flood of June 2016, and is tied for 12th highest documented crest since 1929. This event is between a 5-year flood and a 10-year flood (20%-10% Annual Exceedance Probability) per USGS StreamStats.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southwestern Virginia during the afternoon and evening of June 19. This front interacted with a very unstable environment during the peak heating of the day. The presence of moderate shear allowed an organized line of strong to severe thunderstorms to form and cause damaging thunderstorm winds around much of southwestern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds on Pine Lake Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure spawned showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of June 26th. These thunderstorms were slow moving and produced localized rainfall amounts of two to four inches in an hour or two, which resulted in flash flooding. Another round of showers and thunderstorms developed late in the overnight and into the morning of June 27th bringing additional heavy rain and flooding to Prince William County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kettle Run entered minor flood stage. At this level, a  foot of water is flowing over the lowest point of Valley View Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across the Ohio Valley on June 8. A stationary front attached to the low was located across central to southeast Virginia. Meanwhile, a secondary surface low formed along the stationary front during the evening of June 8. Scattered storms developed during the morning along a remnant MCV and during the afternoon and evening of June 8. These storms produced damaging winds and hail as they moved across portions of central and eastern Virginia. Occasional rotating was also noted as storms interacted with the stationary front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: VDOT reported a tree down across Little Patrick Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough approached the Mid-Atlantic region from the west on June 19th. Strong solar heating and rich low level moisture combined for robust instability across the area. Moderate wind shear ahead of the upper trough resulted in sufficient shear for thunderstorm organization and resulted in widespread damaging winds across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on Chester Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted portions of eastern and central Virginia over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105-115 degrees with high temperatures in the upper 90s to around 100 degrees and dew points in the upper 70s. Dew points increased to around 80 degrees locally in the evenings. Given the duration and magnitude of the heatwave, little relief occurred overnight between each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted Surry County over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105 to 115 degrees with most areas peaking around 110 degrees. Daily high temperatures in the upper 90s and dew points in the mid to upper 70s were also common. Dew points peaked around 80 degrees in some locations.

Additionally, the rain was falling over ground that was partially frozen, and covered in snow and ice from a winter storm that impacted southwest and central Virginia a few days earlier, increasing the rapid runoff potential into the creeks and rivers. In this event, CREST Unit Streamflow did not show impressive returns, while MRMS underestimated some of the precipitation totals. It was those factors combined with the high runoff potential of the rainfall that resulted in a lack of realization how quickly river levels were rising, and just how intense the flooding was becoming, even as far east as Appomattox County. CREST Hydrophobic Unit Flow proved to be a more accurate tool in this situation given the frozen ground, with returns as high as 480 cfs/m^2 across Giles County, which is where some of the most intense flooding was observed. Because MRMS was underestimating rainfall amounts, both the FLASH ARI and QPE/FFG values were also significantly underestimated. In these situation, monitoring ground-truth precipitation totals from a network of private weather stations might have prompted Flash Flood Warnings to be issued sooner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stroubles Creek was observed overflowing its banks into a nearby field and across portions of Stroubles Creek Road a few inches deep.  The road was passible to traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southwestern Virginia during the afternoon and evening of June 19. This front interacted with a very unstable environment during the peak heating of the day. The presence of moderate shear allowed an organized line of strong to severe thunderstorms to form and cause damaging thunderstorm winds around much of southwestern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of about a dozen trees was blown down by thunderstorm winds in an area between Burnt Chimney Road and Brooks Mill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across the Ohio Valley on June 8. A stationary front attached to the low was located across central to southeast Virginia. Meanwhile, a secondary surface low formed along the stationary front during the evening of June 8. Scattered storms developed during the morning along a remnant MCV and during the afternoon and evening of June 8. These storms produced damaging winds and hail as they moved across portions of central and eastern Virginia. Occasional rotating was also noted as storms interacted with the stationary front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: VDOT reported a tree blocking the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough approached the Mid-Atlantic region from the west on June 19th. Strong solar heating and rich low level moisture combined for robust instability across the area. Moderate wind shear ahead of the upper trough resulted in sufficient shear for thunderstorm organization and resulted in widespread damaging winds across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on Carson Ruritan Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted portions of eastern and central Virginia over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105-115 degrees with high temperatures in the upper 90s to around 100 degrees and dew points in the upper 70s. Dew points increased to around 80 degrees locally in the evenings. Given the duration and magnitude of the heatwave, little relief occurred overnight between each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted the City of Virginia Beach over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105 to 115 degrees with most areas peaking around 110 degrees. Daily high temperatures in the upper 90s and dew points in the mid to upper 70s were also common. Dew points peaked around 80 degrees in some locations.

Social Media video indicates that the Well Fields in the town of Saltville flooded out of their banks across Palmer Avenue nearly a foot deep in spots.  The parking lot of Food Country and Subway was also flooded by the Well Fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather outbreak occurred during the mid to late afternoon hours of Thursday, June 19th, 2025, as a strong cold front supported by a seasonably strong upper-level jet intersected a hot, humid, and unstable airmass. Severe weather was exclusively in the form of damaging wind gusts, many of which were considerable (near hurricane-force) in nature in the Washington DC metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, branches, and wires blew down in the City of Waynesboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, humid, and unstable airmass was in position over much of the Mid-Atlantic and southeastern United States. A shortwave trough moving through the Mid-Atlantic brought about more organized convection, especially in the Piedmont of Virginia. Severe thunderstorms caused tree damage in a couple Virginia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds along Sandy Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted portions of eastern and central Virginia over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105-115 degrees with high temperatures in the upper 90s to around 100 degrees and dew points in the upper 70s. Dew points increased to around 80 degrees locally in the evenings. Given the duration and magnitude of the heatwave, little relief occurred overnight between each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted Accomack County over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105 to 115 degrees with most areas peaking around 110 degrees. Daily high temperatures in the upper 90s and dew points in the mid to upper 70s were also common. Dew points peaked around 80 degrees in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough helped power a 150 knot jet streak, which in turn powered isentropic lift, bringing light to heavy snow to portions of southwest Virginia, before an eventual transition to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow measured on snow board at 2600 feet of elevation in Coeburn during a heavy snow band at 4 AM LST. Spotter reported local roads were snow covered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough was observed exiting the southern Rockies during the morning of February 15th, with southwesterly windflow ahead of the trough allowing for a fetch of deep moisture from the western Gulf of America. This moisture pooled along a warm front situated across from Kentucky into southwest Virginia, where precipitable water values increased from 0.6 to 0.7 inches at 7 am that morning, to around 1.1 to 1.2 inches by early afternoon, which would be around 2 standard deviations above normal for mid-February. While the warm front shifted northward to the west of the Appalachians, high pressure off the Atlantic coast that was wedged against the eastern face of the Appalachians delayed the northward progress of the warm front. As such, the front remained stationary, and provided the focus for training of bands of heavy rain that fell across southwest Virginia through much of the day on February 15th. The rain fell at rates of 0.3 to 0.5 per hour beginning at around 9 am EST in the morning, which persisted for a 9-10 hour period. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 3 inches across southwest Virginia during this period. Soils across the area were nearly saturated from 2 rounds of widespread precipitation that fell earlier in the month. Additionally, the rain was falling over ground that was partially frozen, and covered in snow and ice from a winter storm that impacted southwest and central Virginia a few days earlier, increasing the rapid runoff potential into the creeks and rivers. In this event, CREST Unit Streamflow did not show impressive returns, while MRMS underestimated some of the precipitation totals. It was those factors combined with the high runoff potential of the rainfall that resulted in a lack of realization how quickly river levels were rising, and just how intense the flooding was becoming, even as far east as Appomattox County. CREST Hydrophobic Unit Flow proved to be a more accurate tool in this situation given the frozen ground, with returns as high as 480 cfs/m^2 across Giles County, which is where some of the most intense flooding was observed. Because MRMS was underestimating rainfall amounts, both the FLASH ARI and QPE/FFG values were also significantly underestimated. In these situation, monitoring ground-truth precipitation totals from a network of private weather stations might have prompted Flash Flood Warnings to be issued sooner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spruce Run was observed overflowing its banks, and was flowing several inches deep across Spruce Run Road. No damage was reported to the road, which was open to traffic after the flooding receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southwestern Virginia during the afternoon and evening of June 19. This front interacted with a very unstable environment during the peak heating of the day. The presence of moderate shear allowed an organized line of strong to severe thunderstorms to form and cause damaging thunderstorm winds around much of southwestern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds in the area between Stuart and Woolwine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving showers and thunderstorms caused localized flooding across portions of Virginia during the afternoon and evening of June 15, 2025. These showers and thunderstorms were in association with a vigorous upper level shortwave trough and quasi-stationary boundary meandering over the area. A general two to four inches of rain fell across areas where flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were blocked by high water around Craigsville, with 6 to 12 inches of water flowing across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic system with heavy rainfall during warm front passage followed by additional rainfall during the severe thunderstorms along the cold front led to reports of flooding in northern portions of East Tennessee. Additionally, strong winds powered by strong winds aloft mixed to the surface, bringing wind advisory conditions to the whole area, a few high wind reports, and some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on the 15th sent the Powell river near Jonesville that evening, with the river remaining in flood stage until the evening of the 16th. It crested around 28 ft during the early morning hours of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic system with heavy rainfall during warm front passage followed by additional rainfall during the severe thunderstorms along the cold front led to reports of flooding in northern portions of East Tennessee. Additionally, strong winds powered by strong winds aloft mixed to the surface, bringing wind advisory conditions to the whole area, a few high wind reports, and some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on the 15th caused the Clinch river at Cleveland to go into flood stage during the late afternoon hours on the 15th, cresting 20.48 feet on the 16th, and falling back below flood stage during the early morning hours on the 17th. Impact statements state water affects a few houses both on Main Street and adjacent to the ballpark entrance, and approximately 35 acres about two miles up and downstream from the Highway 82 bridge. No damages reported by emergency management.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains on the 15th send the Clinch river at Speers Ferry into minor flood stage during the evening of the 15th. It crested just over moderate flood stage at 28.16 ft on the afternoon of the 16th, then fell back below flood stage on the evening of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted portions of eastern and central Virginia over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105-115 degrees with high temperatures in the upper 90s to around 100 degrees and dew points in the upper 70s. Dew points increased to around 80 degrees locally in the evenings. Given the duration and magnitude of the heatwave, little relief occurred overnight between each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted Chesterfield County over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105 to 115 degrees with most areas peaking around 110 degrees. Daily high temperatures in the upper 90s and dew points in the mid to upper 70s were also common. Dew points peaked around 80 degrees in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system moved from the Four-Corners region east across the states bordering the Gulf of America. This resulted in deep moisture flow riding a southwesterly 50-knot low level jet from the Gulf through the central Appalachians and the upper Ohio River Valley beginning on January 31st, with precipitable water values rapidly increasing into the 1.1 to 1.2 inch range by sunrise across most of the lower Mid-Atlantic. These values were roughly 2 standard deviations above normal for early February. Snow cover prior to the event was confined to the higher terrain of the Alleghany Highlands, with localized snow-water equivalent totals ranging from 1.1 to 1.8 inches. Steady rainfall entered central and western Virginia early on the 31st and persisted through around 7 pm before exiting to the east. Storm total rainfall amounts with this event ranged mainly from 1.75 to 2.50, though with locally higher and lower amounts. Prior to the event, multiple stream gages across the upper James River basin were reporting flows that were below normal, ranging between the 10th to 24th percentile of 28-day average streamflows, though soil moisture was in the normal range. D0: Abnormally Dry conditions were in place per the US Drought Monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clinch River at Richlands (RLRV2) rose to an estimated 11.2 to 11.5 feet on the evening of the 31st, with flooding continuing into the early morning hours of February 1st. An exact crest is uncertain given the gage went out of service during the flooding event. Water was observed covering the ball fields at Critterville Park. Homes off of Alleghany Avenue and 4th Street, as well as along 1st Street, were observed surrounded by water. Some evacuations were conducted as a precaution, however there were no reports of flood waters from the Clinch River entering homes or causing vehicles to become stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A baroclinic cyclone originating near the Gulf and moving by south of Tennessee, combined with a strong upper level jet, provided for two rounds of snow across southwestern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two bouts of snow fell in Lee County per the trained spotter in Jonesville. First round delivered 3.25 inches of snow and came to an end at noon on the 19th. Second round of snow overnight into the morning of the 20th delivered another 1.2 inches, for an event total of 4.35 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to heavy snow fell across Wise County, with the CO-OP observer in Wise reporting 4.7 inches of snow and a trained spotter in Coeburn reporting 5.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two swimmers were caught in a rip current while swimming near the southern end of the Chincoteague Beach Parking Lot at Assateague Island National Seashore on July 24. One was rescued while the other was pulled from the water unconscious and unresponsive and was later pronounced dead upon arrival at a hospital.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two swimmers were caught in a rip current while swimming near the southern end of the Chincoteague Beach parking lot at Assateague Island National Seashore on July 24. One was rescued while the other, an 18 year old male, was pulled from the water unconscious and unresponsive and was later pronounced dead upon arrival at a hospital after CPR was performed. The incident occurred 150 yards south of the lifeguarded zone of the seashore, however, lifeguards responded quickly and assisted with the search.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass was in place from July 14 through July 15. This allowed for widespread, slow-moving storms to move across portions of central, southern, and southeastern Virginia. These storms produced heavy rain and widespread flash flooding in two rounds. One round was from overnight convection on the night of July 14 into the morning of July 15 and the other round was during the afternoon of July 15. Widespread rainfall totals of 2-4 inches were observed across south-central and southern Virginia as well as the Northern Neck with localized totals of 5-7 inches between eastern Dinwiddie County to western Surry County. The highest measured total was 7.33 inches from the Petersburg, Dinwiddie County Airport ASOS/AWOS. Given the very heavy rainfall and saturated soil leading up to the event, a flash flood emergency was issued in Petersburg on the night of July 14 through the morning of July 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported across South Hill. Water depth up to the individual's calves. A mattress was also seen floating down one of the side streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass combined with orographical lift to produce isolated severe thunderstorms across southwest Virginia. Damaging wind gusts from these storms blew down a few trees, and lightning from one of these storms struck a house and set it on fire in Bedford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy runoff associated with nearly 3 inches of rain falling in a 90-minute period caused the Ball Park Road to flood and become washed out near the 2200 block. Radar-estimated rainfall rates at one point during the storms were as high as 6 inches per hour.  FLASH 1-hour ARI rose as high as a 94 year event at 3:10 pm EDT, while the 1-hour QPE/FFG ratio  rose to a maximum of 175 in the nearby area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Virginia Department of Emergency Management in collaboration with the National Weather Service in Wakefield Virginia conducted a Virginia Incident Damage Assessment for the City of Petersburg after significant flooding on the night of July 14 into the morning of July 15. Widespread rainfall totals of 3-5 inches were observed across Petersburg with one report of 7.33 inches from the Petersburg, Dinwiddie County Airport ASOS/AWOS. The damage assessment found 38 damaged commercial buildings with a combined estimated damage of $3,863,288 and 16 damaged residential buildings with an estimated damage of $897,205. There was major damage to 9 commercial properties and 2 residential properties as well as minor damage to 14 commercial properties and 8 residential properties. The remainder of the properties noted above were affected by the flood waters, but were below minor damage criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on July 18th, a cold front approached from the west and then stalled over southeast Ohio and West Virginia. The boundary eventually slid north as a warm front on the 19th of July while a low passed across the Great Lakes region and took aim for New England. Another cold front that trailed behind this secondary system approached from the north and then sank down through the middle Ohio Valley and Central Appalachians on the 21st. During this time, the Middle Ohio Valley and Central Appalachians remained firmly entrenched in a warm, moist air mass that facilitated development of showers and thunderstorms in the daytime followed by lulls in precipitation overnight. ||On the 18th of the month, one to two inches of rainfall led to flash flooding which affected one road and entered multiple homes in Dickenson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water entered multiple houses in West Dante.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sweeping through the region on the first of July set forth damaging wind gusts across southwest Virginia for a brief period of time. Strong thunderstorms progressing ahead of the front ventured into Dickenson County during the middle of the afternoon and promoted two isolated instances of downed trees, one occurring in the town of Nora and the other near Haysi. These storms quickly progressed out of the forecast area due to the fast movement of the cold front, bringing the threat for severe weather to an end by the early evening on the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down at Dante Mountain Road and Stratton Bridge Road in Nora.

One round was from overnight convection on the night of July 14 into the morning of July 15 and the other round was during the afternoon of July 15. Widespread rainfall totals of 2-4 inches were observed across south-central and southern Virginia as well as the Northern Neck with localized totals of 5-7 inches between eastern Dinwiddie County to western Surry County. The highest measured total was 7.33 inches from the Petersburg, Dinwiddie County Airport ASOS/AWOS. Given the very heavy rainfall and saturated soil leading up to the event, a flash flood emergency was issued in Petersburg on the night of July 14 through the morning of July 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee at the Dinwiddie County Airport reported that the taxiway was flooded and  that multiple airplane hangars were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching the Appalachian Mountains combined with a hot and humid air mass to produce scattered severe thunderstorms across southwest Virginia. Damaging wind gusts from these storms blew down several trees and power lines, and lightning from one of these storms struck a house and set it on fire in Buckingham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house along the 1000 block of River Ridge Road and set it on  fire. The fire caused some damage to the house before it was extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved south of the area while cold air remained in place across Virginia. This resulted in a period of snow, sleet, and freezing rain. Snow amounts were generally less than one inch. Ice amounts averaged around a glaze to one tenth of an inch west of US Route 15. Up to two tenths of an inch of ice were reported on the higher ridges of George Washington National Forest, Highland County, and the Blue Ridge.

A thunderstorm developed over the City of Roanoke just before 6:45 pm EDT on August 21st, initially producing rainfall rates to nearly 5 per hour. Though the storm gradually weakened, it remained anchored over primarily the western half of the city through around 8:15 pm EDT before finally pushing away to the south.  While nearly all of the city received at least 2 inches of rain, the highest totals were over the Peters Creek basin, where a 4 to 5 bullseye stretched from the Roanoke-Blacksburg Regional Airport (KROA) westward to S. Electric Road.  Both the airport and a personal weather station observed rainfall totals at the 1-hour and 2-hour durations that were 500-year rainfall events per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates. By 8:00 pm EDT, CREST Unit Streamflow near the airport had peaked at just over 1,600 cfs/mi^2, with much of the western half of the city observing values ranging from 550 to 950 cfs/mi^2. FLASH 1-hour Average Recurrence Intervals peaked near Electric Drive at just under a 200-year event at 7:46 pm EDT.  Due to the rapid flooding of Peters Creek Road that runs alongside Peters Creek, the occupants of numerous vehicles required swift water rescues from emergency services personnel.  A Flash Flood Warning with a Base tag was issued at 7:29 pm EDT for the City of Roanoke, which was upgraded to a warning with a Considerable tag at 7:52 pm EDT when reports of swift water rescues began to come in.  After coordinating with the Emergency Manager of the City of Roanoke, the warning was upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency with a Catastrophic tag at 9:02 pm EDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Roanoke-Blacksburg Regional Airport (KROA) recorded at daily rainfall total of 4.27 inches, most of which fell between 6:50 pm EDT and 8:50 pm EDT.  This was a new daily rainfall record for August 21, surpassing the old record of 1.73 set in 1960. This was also Roanoke's 3rd highest 1-day total for any day in August, and the 13th highest 1-day total on record, with records dating back to February 1912. This total had a nearly a 500-year average recurrence interval for a 2-hour period per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred after the passage of a strong cold front as a pressure gradient increased between a departing low pressure system and a building high pressure system. Gusts of roughly 60 to 65 mph were measured by equipment across some of the higher elevations of southwest Virginia between the early afternoon on the 29th through the pre-dawn hours of the 30th. The strongest measured gust, 65 mph, occurred near Rugby in Grayson County during the early afternoon of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic gusts of 58 mph or greater occurred one mile southeast of Check from the evening of the 29th trough the morning of the 30th. The strongest gust of 63 mph occurred at 11:53 PM EST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture on social media showed high water reaching the tops of tires on parked cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-summer hot and humid airmass was in place across most of Virginia on August 14. Additionally, a slow-moving cold front stalled over central and southeast Virginia in the afternoon, supporting thunderstorms with heavy rain and flash flooding in southeast Virginia during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that North Pine Grove Road was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving line of storms passed across the City of Galax and neighboring Grayson County just before 2 pm EDT during the afternoon of August 1st, with multiple cells within the line observed training across the city. The line weakened and exited the area by around 3:30 pm EDT.  Rainfall rates were observed to be as high as 4.5 inches per hour in a couple areas of the city, with storm total amounts per multiple personal weather stations observed to be between 3 and 3.25 inches.  These amounts are estimated to be around a 75-year event at the 90 minute duration per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates. CREST Unit Streamflow reached a maximum of a little over 500 cfs per mi^2, while the FLASH 1-hr QPE/FFG ratio reached as high as 150.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals ranging from 3 to 3.25 inches occurred during about a 90-minute period, with rain falling at rates exceeding 4 inches per hour. Runoff from this rain caused flooding on multiple roads in and around the City of Galax. Reports of flooding include heavy runoff from the rain flowing into a nursing and rehab center along Painter Street.  Several inches of water were also observed flowing across Oldtown Street, Glendale Road, Bedsaul Road, Parkwood Drive and Webster Street in front of Twin County Tire Company.  No damage was reported to the roadways, which were open to traffic once the flooding receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving line of storms passed across the City of Galax and neighboring Grayson County just before 2 pm EDT during the afternoon of August 1st, with multiple cells within the line observed training across the city. The line weakened and exited the area by around 3:30 pm EDT.  Rainfall rates were observed to be as high as 4.5 inches per hour in a couple areas of the city, with storm total amounts per multiple personal weather stations observed to be between 3 and 3.25 inches.  These amounts are estimated to be around a 75-year event at the 90 minute duration per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates. CREST Unit Streamflow reached a maximum of a little over 500 cfs per mi^2, while the FLASH 1-hr QPE/FFG ratio reached as high as 150.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station (Weather Underground: KVAGALAX3) recorded a rainfall total of 3.14 inches which fell during a 90-minute period.  This estimates to be roughly a 75-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates for this time duration.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours in an atmosphere rich with high moisture values. This moisture was realized as locally heavy rainfall. One thunderstorm in Rockbridge County had enough heavy rain in a concentrated area to bring damaging wind gusts to the surface, downing around thirty trees near Rockbridge Baths.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed around thirty trees within the 800 block of Mt. Atlas Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred after the passage of a strong cold front as a pressure gradient increased between a departing low pressure system and a building high pressure system. Gusts of roughly 60 to 65 mph were measured by equipment across some of the higher elevations of southwest Virginia between the early afternoon on the 29th through the pre-dawn hours of the 30th. The strongest measured gust, 65 mph, occurred near Rugby in Grayson County during the early afternoon of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic wind gust of 58 mph or greater occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th within one mile east of Mountain Lake. The strongest gust of 64 mph occurred at 5:30 AM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the region during the early morning hours of the 19th. Behind this front, strong to damaging west to northwest winds were reported, and/or blew down trees. The strongest reported wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds, occurring behind an exiting cold front, blew down a tree near the intersection of Mount Chestnut Road and Route 221. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of an upper level disturbance, supported by abundant sub-freezing air near the surface, allowed for the development of a widespread area of snow. Snow began falling during the morning hours on the 8th, and concluded during the late afternoon to early evening. Within this broader region of snow, heavier snow bands developed that allowed for locally higher amounts. Across portions of southwest and south-central Virginia, snowfall amounts ranged from three to eight inches. The high end of this range occurred across the higher elevations of Grayson County. Additionally, a swath of around six inches of snow occurred from roughly Bedford County, Virginia east through portions of the James River Valley region of Virginia. Key Impacts: Hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall occurred across the county during the morning hours through the late evening of the 8th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 3.3 inches at a location two miles south of Pilot to 6.5 inches at a location two miles south-southeast of Check. Key Impacts: Hazardous travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure tracked across the southeastern section of the United States of America, moisture from this system interacted with cold air over southwest Virginia. The result was an area of snow which blanketed parts of the state during the pre-dawn and early morning hours of the 5th with as much as five inches of snow. The heaviest snows fell over Amherst and adjacent Bedford Counties. Key Impact: Hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across the county ranged from 3.0 inches to around 4.0 inches across the county, though less than half of the county received 4 inches. Key Impacts: Hazardous travel.

A coastal low formed off of the Southeast coast on October 11 and slowly tracked north into October 13 before moving offshore. Meanwhile, a strong 1035mb area of high pressure lingered over the Gulf of Saint Lawrence. This resulted in a prolonged period of strong northeast to north winds across the Chesapeake Bay and coastal waters, which allowed for widespread moderate to major coastal flooding across eastern Virginia and the Virginia Eastern Shore on October 12 into October 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingering off the Southeast coast on October 12 resulted in a prolonged period of elevated northeast to north winds across the Chesapeake Bay. This led to widespread moderate to major coastal flooding across Mathews County on October 12 into October 13. Windmill Point reached 4.25 ft MLLW at 318 PM EST on October 12, the seventh highest crest at that site, and 3.71 ft at 348 AM EST on October 13. Gywnns Island reached 4.13 ft MLLW at 155 PM EST on October 12 and 3.51 ft MLLW at 345 AM EST on October 13. The Ware River reached 5.54 ft MLLW at 1230 PM EST on October 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low formed off of the Southeast coast on October 11 and slowly tracked north into October 13 before moving offshore. Meanwhile, a strong 1035mb area of high pressure lingered over the Gulf of Saint Lawrence. This resulted in a prolonged period of strong northeast to north winds across the Chesapeake Bay and coastal waters, which allowed for widespread moderate to major coastal flooding across eastern Virginia and the Virginia Eastern Shore on October 12 into October 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingering off the Southeast coast on October 12 resulted in a prolonged period of elevated northeast to north winds across the Chesapeake Bay. This led to widespread moderate to major coastal flooding across James City County on October 12 into October 13. Yorktown reached 5.66 ft MLLW at 136 PM EST on October 12 which was the nineth highest crest ever at that site. Jamestown reached 5.51 ft MLLW at 448 PM EST on October 12, which was the second highest crest at that site, and 4.59 ft at 412 AM EST on October 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed offshore of the Southeast coast in the early morning of October 28. This system lifted northeastward along a frontal boundary later in the day, while strengthening some. High pressure north of the region led to a compressed pressure gradient with gusty winds across eastern Virginia for most of the day. Higher wind gusts were confined to the areas near the coast and peak gusts were in the 45-50 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An offshore low pressure system brought gusty northeast winds to coastal areas October 28. Peak observed wind gusts included a 51 mph (44 knot) gust at the Cape Henry (XHEN) mesonet station and a 49 mph (43 knot) gust at the Oceana Naval Air Station (KNTU) AWOS station. Winds decreased in the evening hours.

This led to moderate coastal flooding across the city of Suffolk on October 12 into October 13. Sleepy Hole Park reached 7.30 ft MLLW at 230 PM EST on October 12. Sewell's Point reached 6.18 ft MLLW at 130 PM EST on October 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low formed off of the Southeast coast on October 11 and slowly tracked north into October 13 before moving offshore. Meanwhile, a strong area of high pressure lingered over the Gulf of Saint Lawrence. This resulted in a prolonged period of strong northeast winds across southeast Virginia on October 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds developed due to a coastal low lingering offshore on October 12. Winds frequently gusted to 40-50 mph between 5 AM and 9 PM EST on October 12. The strongest winds occurred from 9 AM to 3 PM. Virginia Beach had a maximum wind gust of 51 mph at the Cape Henry NOS site around 11 AM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swimmer drowned in the water off of Virginia Beach on October 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 23 year old male was swimming with friends when he began signaling for help. A bystander tried to rescue him but was unsuccessful. Virginia Beach Police responded but were unable to locate the swimmer until his body was recovered the following day. While the exact cause of drowning is unclear, there was a high risk for rip currents that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricanes Humberto and Imelda were located well offshore of the Eastern Seaboard on October 1, with high pressure over the Great Lakes and southern Canada. The combination of elevated winds and long-period swell led to coastal flooding. A few locations along the Virginia tidal rivers reached moderate flood stage from later October 1 into early October 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevated winds and long-period swell led to coastal flooding in eastern Virginia, particularly on the tidal rivers. The tide gauge at Tappahannock, VA exceeded moderate flood threshold two times: recording 3.50 feet MLLW at 10:05 PM EST on October 1 and 3.57 feet MLLW at 10:00 PM EST on October 2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevated winds and long-period swell led to coastal flooding in eastern Virginia, particularly on the tidal rivers. The tide gauge at Windmill Point, VA recorded moderate flooding with a reading of 3.51 feet MLLW at 7:00 AM EST on October 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricanes Humberto and Imelda were located well offshore of the Eastern Seaboard on October 1, with high pressure over the Great Lakes and southern Canada. The combination of elevated winds and long-period swell led to coastal flooding. A few locations along the Virginia tidal rivers reached moderate flood stage from later October 1 into early October 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevated winds and long-period swell led to coastal flooding in eastern Virginia, particularly on the tidal rivers. The tide gauge at Windmill Point, VA recorded moderate flooding with a reading of 3.51 feet MLLW at 7:00 AM EST on October 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low formed off of the Southeast coast on October 11 and slowly tracked north into October 13 before moving offshore. Meanwhile, a strong 1035mb area of high pressure lingered over the Gulf of Saint Lawrence. This resulted in a prolonged period of strong northeast to north winds across the Chesapeake Bay and coastal waters, which allowed for widespread moderate to major coastal flooding across eastern Virginia and the Virginia Eastern Shore on October 12 into October 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingering off the Southeast coast on October 12 resulted in a prolonged period of elevated northeast to north winds across the Chesapeake Bay. This led to moderate coastal flooding across Westmoreland County on October 12. Lewisetta reached 3.97 ft MLLW at 524 PM EST on October 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed offshore of the Southeast coast in the early morning of October 28. This system lifted northeastward along a frontal boundary later in the day, while strengthening some. High pressure north of the region led to a compressed pressure gradient with gusty winds across eastern Virginia for most of the day. Higher wind gusts were confined to the areas near the coast and peak gusts were in the 45-50 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An offshore low pressure system brought gusty northeast winds to coastal areas October 28. A peak observed wind gust of 46 mph (40 knots) was recorded at the Norfolk International Airport (KORF) ASOS station. Winds decreased in the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the Mid-Atlantic coast combined with high pressure in Canada, resulting in a lengthy period of northeasterly onshore flow. Increasing tidal anomalies resulted in moderate to locally major tidal flooding over an extended period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds between coastal low pressure offshore and high pressure over eastern Canada resulted in an extended period of coastal flooding. The gauge at Lynnhaven Inlet recorded moderate coastal flooding of 5.26 ft MLLW 1:30 PM EDT on October 28th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingering off the Southeast coast on October 12 resulted in a prolonged period of elevated northeast to north winds across the Chesapeake Bay. This led to moderate coastal flooding across the city of Chesapeake on October 12 into October 13. Sewell's Point reached 6.18 ft MLLW at 130 PM EST on October 12. Money Point reached 6.59 ft MLLW at 206 PM EST on October 12. The Western Branch of the Elizabeth River reached 6.52 ft MLLW at 2 PM EST on October 12 and the Elizabeth River at Midtown Tunnel reached 6.50 ft MLLW at 154 PM EST on October 12. Additionally, the intersection of Bainbridge Boulevard an Freeman Avenue was nearly impassable due to approximately 18 to 24 inches of water across the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingering off the Southeast coast on October 12 resulted in a prolonged period of elevated northeast to north winds across the Chesapeake Bay. This led to moderate to major coastal flooding across the city of Norfolk on October 12 into October 13. Sewell's Point reached 6.18 ft MLLW at 130 PM EST on October 12. Money Point reached 6.59 ft MLLW at 206 PM EST on October 12. Lynnhaven Inlet reached 6.17 ft MLLW at 1 PM EST on October 12 which was the second highest crest ever recorded at that site. Lynnhaven also reached 5.37 ft MLLW at 1226 AM on October 13 and 5.21 ft MLLW at 130 PM EST on October 13. Additionally, the Western Branch of the Elizabeth River reached 6.52 ft MLLW at 2 PM EST on October 12 and the Elizabeth River at Midtown Tunnel reached 6.50 ft MLLW at 154 PM EST on October 12. Water was reported across Riverside Drive in Portsmouth. In Norfolk, an emergency manager reported coastal flooding off Pleasant Avenue in neighborhoods along Little Creek, with 18th Bay Street mostly inundated at high tide. Additionally, approximately two feet of water was reported by the public at the intersection of Delaware Avenue and Llewellyn Avenue with multiple cars reported to be stalled out due to the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low formed off of the Southeast coast on October 11 and slowly tracked north into October 13 before moving offshore. Meanwhile, a strong area of high pressure lingered over the Gulf of Saint Lawrence. This resulted in a prolonged period of strong northeast winds across southeast Virginia on October 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds developed due to a coastal low lingering offshore on October 12. Winds frequently gusted to 40-50 mph between 8 AM and 10 PM EST on October 12. Norfolk had a peak gust to 48 mph at the Norfolk Naval Station (KNGU) and 47 mph at Norfolk International Airport (KORF).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low formed off of the Southeast coast on October 11 and slowly tracked north into October 13 before moving offshore. Meanwhile, a strong area of high pressure lingered over the Gulf of Saint Lawrence. This resulted in a prolonged period of strong northeast winds across southeast Virginia on October 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds developed due to a coastal low lingering offshore on October 12. Winds frequently gusted to 40-50 mph between 7 AM and 5 PM EST on October 12. There was a peak gust to 53 mph at the Wachapreague NOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred after the passage of a strong cold front as a pressure gradient increased between a departing low pressure system and a building high pressure system. Gusts of roughly 60 to 65 mph were measured by equipment across some of the higher elevations of southwest Virginia between the early afternoon on the 29th through the pre-dawn hours of the 30th. The strongest measured gust, 65 mph, occurred near Rugby in Grayson County during the early afternoon of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted 59 to 65 mph across portions of Grayson County during the early afternoon of the 29th between Whitetop and Rugby.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts at or above 58 mph occurred around one mile north of Bent Mountain from the early afternoon of the 29th through the late evening. The strongest gust of 63 mph occurred at 10:09 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved south of the area while cold air remained in place across Virginia. This resulted in a period of snow, sleet, and freezing rain. Snow amounts were generally less than one inch. Ice amounts averaged around a glaze to one tenth of an inch west of US Route 15. Up to two tenths of an inch of ice were reported on the higher ridges of George Washington National Forest, Highland County, and the Blue Ridge.

During the early morning hours of December 19th, a strong cold front progressed through southwestern Virginia and caused a quick burst in strong wind gusts. Downed trees and power lines in the midst of the elevated winds were first noted around one in the morning, and remained the case until winds subsided around daybreak. Nearly 5,000 homes and businesses in Buchanan and Dickenson Counties were without power due to this event. High pressure building into the western Ohio Valley allowed winds to subside quickly as the front dashed towards the eastern seaboard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the Mid-Atlantic bringing a period of snow to portions of the Allegheny Mountains, Shenandoah Valley and Central Virginia. Snow began in the predawn and spread east through the morning. Steady snow continued through the day before tapering to lighter snow during the evening hours. Snowfall accumulations of two to six inches were observed, with upwards of eight inches in Highland County and across the higher peaks of the Blue Ridge.
