A warm front brought a period of elevation dependent snow to western Highland County during the daylight hours of December 10th. During the evening, a cold front moved through changing all precipitation to snow. Bursts of snow continued through the overnight before tapering to snow showers by daybreak December 11th. Snowfall accumulations of two to eight inches were observed. Winds gusted to 40 mph resulting in blowing snow. The combination of blowing and falling snow reduced visibilities below one quarter of a mile at at times.

A cold front crossed the region during the early morning hours of the 19th. Behind this front, strong to damaging west to northwest winds were reported, and/or blew down trees. The strongest reported wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site recorded a wind gust of 63 mph at 09:50 AM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the region during the early morning hours of the 19th. Behind this front, strong to damaging west to northwest winds were reported, and/or blew down trees. The strongest reported wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site recorded a 64 mph wind gust at a location around three miles east of Byllesby at 1:14 PM through 1:19 PM EST. Prior to that happening, a tree was blown down on a power line at a location five miles north of Dugspur at 10:05 AM EST. A second tree was blown down four miles northwest of Laurel Fork at 11:36 AM EST. Damage values are estimated.

Elevated winds of around 35 to 40 miles per hour prevailed over Buchanan and Dickenson Counties through that evening before subsiding in response to building high pressure from the west.

A moisture rich Alberta Clipper moved into the Appalachians during the late afternoon of December 31. Ahead of the snow, light freezing drizzle developed resulting in a glaze of ice. Light to moderate developed through the late afternoon and continued steadily through the evening. Just after midnight, a snow squall along an Arctic Front moved through providing a quick coating to half inch of snow. Total snow accumulations for the event were two to five inches. Winds gusted to 45 mph resulting in significant blowing and drifting snow.

A quick hitting winter storm swept across southwest Virginia on December 8th and brought light snow accumulations to the area. Snow showers arrived just before sunrise and prevailed into the evening before departing eastward. Members of the local Department of Highways for both Buchanan and Dickenson Counties estimated anywhere between 2 to 3.5 inches of snow fell across the area, with the higher amounts located along the mountainous terrain. High pressure built into the area in the wake of the passing system, bringing drier weather back into the region late that night into the next day.

As low pressure tracked across the southeastern section of the United States of America, moisture from this system interacted with cold air over southwest Virginia. The result was an area of snow which blanketed parts of the state during the pre-dawn and early morning hours of the 5th with as much as five inches of snow. The heaviest snows fell over Amherst and adjacent Bedford Counties. Key Impact: Hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall across the county ranged from 4.0 inches at a location three miles southwest of Falconerville to 4.8 inches at a location one mile east-northeast of Amherst. Key Impacts: Hazardous travel.

A quick hitting clipper system embedded within an active stretch of weather for the middle of December swept through southwest Virginia on the morning of the 12th. Snow showers persisted into the middle of the day before activity quieted down as the disturbance dashed to the east. An estimated 2 to 3 inches of snow fell in spots across Buchanan and Dickenson Counties with this transient system, which was noted by members of the Department of Highways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county ranged from 3.0 inches measured by the Cooperative Weather Observer in the Town of Buckingham to 4.3 inches measured near Andersonville.  At least half of the county received 4 inches. Key Impacts: Hazardous travel.

On the evening of November 10th, a passing arctic cold front ushering in colder temperatures caused rain to change over to snow across southwest Virginia, setting forth the first winter weather event of the 2025-2026 season. This was a short-lived event for Buchanan and Dickenson Counties, as snow showers wrapped up around daybreak on the morning of the 11th. Snowfall totals of less than an inch were measured with this event in southwest Virginia.

On the back side of a cold frontal passage, gusty winds took shape across southwest Virginia due to a tightened pressure gradient on November 16th. Winds throughout the day of 30 to 40 miles per hour were observed across the region. One minor injury occurred due to the stronger winds near Clintwood due to a tree falling onto an occupied car. Winds quickly eased in intensity later that evening into the next day once the pressure gradient began to relax overhead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a truck on Herndon Lane near Dante Mountain Road outside of Clintwood. One person inside the car at the time of the fallen tree sustained minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light post frontal snowfall fell across portions of southwestern Virginia on November 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per a trained spotter, 1 inch of snow fell near Jonesville in a 30 minute period before 4 pm local time during a squall. Event total snow amounts totaled 1.6 inches of snow near Jonesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light post frontal snowfall fell across portions of southwestern Virginia on November 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in Wise County, with 5.1 inches of snow measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary sagged south toward the area during the afternoon/evening. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms initially developed to the north (near that boundary) and also to the west along higher terrain. The environment ahead of the boundary was characterized by very weak deep-layer flow and anomalously high PW values on the order of 2.1-2.2, leading to slow-moving storms with very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lieutenant Run Creek at Bollingbrook Street in Petersburg crested at 15.98 feet. The creek rose over 10 feet in 2 hours and 15 minutes.

Scattered to numerous thunderstorms initially developed to the north (near that boundary) and also to the west along higher terrain. The environment ahead of the boundary was characterized by very weak deep-layer flow and anomalously high PW values on the order of 2.1-2.2, leading to slow-moving storms with very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brickhouse Run was overflowing Hinton street and encroached on homes. This resulted in a water rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A one-year-old boy, who would have turned two years old in two days, was left alone and restrained in the back seat of a car in Madison Heights of Amherst County while the foster dad of this child was at work for eight hours. Temperatures rose into the upper 80s by midday with the nearby Lynchburg ASOS reaching a high of 89 degrees. The boy was found in the late afternoon and pulled out of the car but was pronounced dead at a hospital due to succumbing from the heat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Test message.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms pass across portions of Carroll County and the City of Galax during the afternoon of the 19th, with rainfall rates occasionally spiking to nearly 6 inches per hour. Among the reports of flooding that occurred in this area, a small tributary of Chestnut Creek over flowed its banks and flooded several inches deep across a portion of Cliffview Road. Dixon Branch was also observed overflowing its banks across part of Fries Road.  A portion of Hebron Road was flooded and became impassible for a short period of time by flooding of Little Cranberry Creek, and Mill Creek overflowed its banks across Glade Street.  No damage was reported to the roads, which were open to traffic after the water receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary approaching from the north combined with a hot and humid air mass to produce scattered severe thunderstorms across southwest Virginia. Damaging wind gusts from these storms blew down several trees and power lines with one of the trees falling on to a house in Bedford County, and hail up to the size of quarters fell in Bedford and Henry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred on Wards Mill Branch creek, as multiple rounds of heavy rain during thunderstorm activity passed across the basin. Rainfall rates were estimated by radar to be in excess of 4 inches per hour, with totals of 3 to 3.5 inches occurring during a period lasting a little over an hour. This amount is roughly a 100-year event for a 1-hour duration per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates. FLASH 3-hour ARI maxed out at a 50-year event at 6:10 pm EDT, while the FLASH 3-hour QPE/FFG ratio had reached 180. Several structures became surrounded by fast moving water. This resulted in swift water rescues of nine people and four pets in the 100 block of Old Quaker Road. There were also evacuations of a nearby mobile home park on Macy Drive. A downstream river gage, Chestnut Creek at Galax, rose over 4 feet. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down one tree along the 6000 block of Douthat State Park Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across eastern Virginia during the evening of November 9. A seasonally moist and unstable airmass was in place ahead of the cold front, allowing for locally strong to severe storms. One storm produced a waterspout which moved onshore as a tornado in Middlesex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A velocity enhancement from a severe storm crossing the Piankatank River likely formed a waterspout over the river before moving onshore of Bland Point in Deltaville around 935 PM EST. A large oak tree fell on and caused damage to a pool and pool furniture. As the tornado moved almost due east, it twisted and snapped several trees|and large branches. Some pine tree branches were blown into a nearby house, breaking two large windows and a wooden window frame. The wind was strong enough to embed small pine twigs and branches into the stucco siding of the house. Additionally, a picnic table at this property was broken into pieces and thrown roughly 20 to 30 feet. A patch of shingles was damaged from a garage attached to another house at the apex of Bland Point. At this location, a sliding glass door frame was pushed inward. Additionally, balcony furniture was pushed hard enough to bend and partially dislodge the metal railing on the second story. Just outside of the house, 20 heavy duty pool chairs, some made out of polywood and some made out of aluminum, were lofted and thrown around|40 yards. Most of the chairs were broken or shattered. Debris from the pool furniture was thrown hard enough to dent the bark of a pine tree more than 50 yards from the pool. At this point, it appeared that the tornado turned to the northeast, following the shoreline. The winds were strong enough to rip a large metal sheet attached to an in-ground pool cover off of the pool and throw it into 2000 pound marble pillars nearby with enough force to both bend the metal at nearly a 90 degree angle and knock over a couple pillars. To the southeast of the pillars, several wooden planks were blown off of a wooden dock. The tornado then tracked likely along or just offshore of the shoreline for around 220 yards, twisting and snapping several additional trees, before dissipating around 936 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across the Mid-Atlantic early on July 2. In the afternoon, the front slowly advanced southward, serving as a focus for showers and storms through the day. Repeated training of showers and storms led to a significant flood event in rural southern Virginia in the late morning and early afternoon hours. In the late afternoon, a stationary shower produced localized, urban flash flooding in the city of Norfolk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: VDOT reported water over Dry Bread Road with lane closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass combined with orographical lift to produce a severe thunderstorm in Bath County of southwest Virginia. Damaging wind gusts from this storm blew down two trees west of Nimrod Hall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms produced a radar-estimated 4 to 4.25 inches of rain within a 2-hour period in the vicinity of the Towns of Richlands and Cedar Bluff, with most of the rain falling in around 90 minutes.  Such a rainfall would be a 200-year ARI at the 2-hour duration per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates.  Rainfall rates estimated by radar reached a maximum of around 5.5 inches per hour. FLASH 3-hour ARI reached a maximum of a 200-year event by 10:40 pm EDT, while the 3-hour FLASH QPE/FFG Ratio rose as high as 245.  Runoff from the rainfall caused several roads to be flooded and impassible until the flood waters receded within the area.  This included Franklin Avenue and Business Route 460 in Richlands. No known damage resulted from this flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across the Mid-Atlantic early on July 2. In the afternoon, the front slowly advanced southward, serving as a focus for showers and storms through the day. Repeated training of showers and storms led to a significant flood event in rural southern Virginia in the late morning and early afternoon hours. In the late afternoon, a stationary shower produced localized, urban flash flooding in the city of Norfolk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: VDOT reported water on Dry Bread Road near Harris Crossroads with lane closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary sagged south toward the area during the afternoon/evening. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms initially developed to the north (near that boundary) and also to the west along higher terrain. The environment ahead of the boundary was characterized by very weak deep-layer flow and anomalously high PW values on the order of 2.1-2.2, leading to slow-moving storms with very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Traffic cameras showed a large amount of water on I-64 near Northampton Boulevard. Multiple reports of flooding were received via Waze in same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front was located north of the region and gradually moved south in response to an approaching mid level disturbance. To the south of the front, robust heating of a moist low level airmass resulted in increasing instability by the afternoon and evening hours. Strong, slow moving, convection resulted in areas of strong winds, heavy rainfall, and flash flooding during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported flooding on East Rochambeau Drive and along I-64.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted portions of eastern and central Virginia over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105-115 degrees with high temperatures in the upper 90s to around 100 degrees and dew points in the upper 70s. Dew points increased to around 80 degrees locally in the evenings. Given the duration and magnitude of the heatwave, little relief occurred overnight between each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted the cities of Norfolk and Portsmouth over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105 to 115 degrees with most areas peaking around 110 degrees. Daily high temperatures in the upper 90s and dew points in the mid to upper 70s were also common. Dew points peaked around 80 degrees in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass was in place on September 27. Meanwhile, a surface low lingered across south central Virginia along the North Carolina border with a stationary front extending east along the state border. This low slowly moved east-northeast through the afternoon on September 27. This allowed for rounds of heavy showers and storms to move across central and southeast Virginia beginning during the morning hours of September 27 and continuing into the evening, resulting in locally heavy rainfall and scattered instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several waze reports of flash flooding on I-64 near Woodlands Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold front brought a line of showers and thunderstorms that were able to successfully transport strong to severe wind gusts to the surface in portions of southwestern Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure brought gusty winds and heavy rainfall to southeast Virginia and the Virginia Eastern Shore on September 16. Multiple bands of heavy rainfall rotated inland as the low approached the coast from the late morning into the evening hours, with widespread reports of flooding throughout the region during the day. Higher-than-normal high tide cycles also exacerbated impacts from freshwater flooding. Peak storm total rainfall amounts were in the 4-9 range across southside Hampton Roads, including 7.72 at the Oceana Naval Air Station (KNTU) AWOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at the intersection of Oceana Boulevard and Virginia Beach Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingered near the North Carolina and Virginia coastline on September 16, slowly moving northwest into the Chesapeake Bay by the evening of September 16. This low brought a prolonged period of elevated northerly winds across the Chesapeake Bay, resulting in widespread moderate to locally major coastal flooding across portions of eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingered near the North Carolina and Virginia coastline on September 16, slowly moving northwest into the Chesapeake Bay by the evening of September 16. This low brought a prolonged period of elevated northerly winds across the Chesapeake Bay, resulting in moderate coastal flooding in Hampton and Poquoson. Sewells Point reached 5.58 ft MLLW at 454 PM EST on September 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure brought gusty winds to coastal portions of southeast Virginia and the Virginia Eastern Shore September 15 into 16. On September 15, the low pressure system was located offshore and southeast of the area, with brief gusty winds to around 45 mph along the immediate coast in the afternoon due to a tightening pressure gradient. Wind gusts dropped below 40 mph from the evening into the early morning hours of September 16. However, winds increased substantially and peaked in the afternoon and evening hours of September 16 as the low tracked northwest into the lower Chesapeake Bay and eventually moved inland. Widespread wind gusts of 40-50 mph were observed along the Virginia Beach, Norfolk, and Eastern Shore coasts, with peak wind gusts to around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increasingly gusty winds developed as a compact, but deepening, low pressure system approached the Virginia coast on September 15 into 16. A brief period of wind gusts to around 45 mph occurred along the immediate Virginia Beach coast in the afternoon of September 15. Winds then occasionally gusted over 45 mph starting in the morning hours of September 16, with frequent gusts over 45 mph in the afternoon and evening. Peak observed wind gusts included a 60 mph (52 knot) gust at the Cape Henry (XHEN) mesonet station and a 55 mph (48 knot) gust a few miles inland at the Oceana Naval Air Station (KNTU) AWOS station. Winds quickly decreased later in the evening and especially overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingered near the North Carolina and Virginia coastline on September 16, slowly moving northwest into the Chesapeake Bay by the evening of September 16. This low brought a prolonged period of elevated northerly winds across the Chesapeake Bay, resulting in widespread moderate to locally major coastal flooding across portions of eastern Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coastal low lingered near the North Carolina and Virginia coastline on September 16, slowly moving northwest into the Chesapeake Bay by the evening of September 16. This low brought a prolonged period of elevated northerly winds across the Chesapeake Bay, resulting in major coastal flooding across Virginia Beach. The Chesapeake Bay Bridge Tunnel reached 6.14 ft MLLW at 354 PM EST on September 16. Lynnhaven Inlet reached 6.06 ft MLLW at 412 PM EST on September 16. Additionally, there was a report of two to three feet of seawater in Virginia Beach, resulting in stranded and submerged vehicles in the Linkhorn Park area of Virginia Beach just off of Pacific Avenue at 425 PM EST. Another report was received at 357 PM EST indicating one to two feet of seawater flooding in the Wolfsnare subdivision of Virginia Beach with neighborhood roads impassable.

Sewells Point reached 5.58 ft MLLW at 454 PM EST on September 16, the Western Branch of the Elizabeth River reached 6.02 ft MLLW at 442 PM EST on September 16, and Money Point reached 6.08 ft MLLW at 506 PM EST on September 16. Additionally, one to two feet of seawater was reported along Hampton Boulevard in Norfolk, resulting in partially submerged and stranded vehicles at 504 PM EST. Another report was received at 447 PM EST and indicated that one to two feet of seawater resulted in partially submerged vehicles in Old Towne Portsmouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary located in southern Virginia interacted with a very unstable environment to produce severe thunderstorms in south-central Virginia. The thunderstorm winds from these storms caused tree and power line damage.  Moisture was abundant across the area, with precipitable water values observed at between 1.7 and 1.9 inches.  Storms were progressive however, and observed moving to the east at 15 to 20 mph, which did not allow them to remain situated over any single location for long.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a tree and a power pole on Little Mountain Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over eastern Craig County at around 4:00 PM EDT and quickly spread eastward over the Catawba Creek basin in neighboring Botetourt County. Multiple cells trained over this area through around 6:45 PM EDT before exiting to the south.  These storms formed along a stationary front situated across central Virginia. Convergence along the front provided focus for initiation of the storms, which were supported with surface-based CAPEs exceeding 2,000 J/Kg, and precipitable water values in the 1.7 to 1.8 inch range, which are nearly 2 standard deviations above normal for mid-June. By 5:00 PM EDT, roughly 2 inches of rain had fallen across eastern Craig County, with no impressive response in MRMS FLASH data.  An additional 2 to 2.5 inches fell by 6:00 PM EDT however, with FLASH 3-hour ARI indicating at 200+-year event across eastern Craig County. Surprisingly, there did not appear to be any flash flooding warning issued for this event. By 7:00 PM EDT, the 3-hour QPE/FFG ratio indicated that rainfall totals were slightly over 3 times the 3-hour flash flood guidance for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Virginia Department of Transportation was dispatched to Caldwell Mountain Road to address damage caused to the road due to flash flooding.  The road was rendered impassible until repairs could be completed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough approached the Mid-Atlantic region from the west on June 19th. Strong solar heating and rich low level moisture combined for robust instability across the area. Moderate wind shear ahead of the upper trough resulted in sufficient shear for thunderstorm organization and resulted in widespread damaging winds across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree in Wyndham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted portions of eastern and central Virginia over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105-115 degrees with high temperatures in the upper 90s to around 100 degrees and dew points in the upper 70s. Dew points increased to around 80 degrees locally in the evenings. Given the duration and magnitude of the heatwave, little relief occurred overnight between each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heatwave impacted Louisa County over the course of three days from June 23 to 25. Each day, heat indices reached 105 to 115 degrees with most areas peaking around 110 degrees. Daily high temperatures in the upper 90s and dew points in the mid to upper 70s were also common. Dew points peaked around 80 degrees in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Test message.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Doe Run creek was observed flowing out of its banks several inches deep across both Doe Run Road and Ashpone Tavern Road. These roads were impassible until the flooding receded. MRMS Crest Unit Streamflow indicated flows of nearly 840 cfs per square mile at this location at 12:00 am EDT on the 17th, while the 3-hour ARI was maxed out at a 200-year event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Test message.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six Mile Post Road was observed flooded and impassible as Hatchet Creek was observed flooding several inches out of its banks over the roadway.  No damage was reported to the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass was in place on September 27. Meanwhile, a surface low lingered across south central Virginia along the North Carolina border with a stationary front extending east along the state border. This low slowly moved east-northeast through the afternoon on September 27. This allowed for rounds of heavy showers and storms to move across central and southeast Virginia beginning during the morning hours of September 27 and continuing into the evening, resulting in locally heavy rainfall and scattered instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: VDOT reported high water along VA-40 near Oakdale Road.
